Metadata.Coram: Sundaresh Menon CJ, Steven Chong JCA, Belinda Ang Saw Ean JCA, Kannan Ramesh JAD and Judith Prakash SJ
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: OP3 International Pte Ltd (in liquidation)

Facts.Text: OP3 International Pte Ltd was incorporated in December 2006 as a company in Singapore.
OP3 International Pte Ltd carried on business in interior design, decorating consultancy and construction services.
From incorporation, OP3 International Pte Ltd had Mr Foo Kian Beng as its sole director and shareholder.

On 19 July 2013, OP3 International Pte Ltd entered into a contract with Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd to provide fitting out works at one of Smile Inc's clinics for a contract sum of $158,010, with 50% payable on execution and the balance payable upon completion of the works.

Under the contract dated 19 July 2013 between OP3 International Pte Ltd and Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd, the contractually agreed date of completion of the fitting out works was 11 September 2013.
On or about 31 October 2013, OP3 International Pte Ltd completed the fitting out works at the Smile Inc clinic and handed the clinic over to Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd.
On 9 January 2014, Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd observed mould growing on the walls of the clinic that had been fitted out by OP3 International Pte Ltd.
On 17 January 2024, Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd discovered that the mould was the result of a flood in the clinic and notified OP3 of it on the same day.

Upon learning of the First Flood, OP3 undertook rectification works at the Clinic, including assessing that water from the Clinic’s sinks and spittoons was not draining properly and that the flooding occurred at the Clinic’s drainage sump area.
On 20 February 2014, a contractor engaged by OP3 International Pte Ltd conducted tests at the clinic to check for leakages from the sump and water pipes.
On 21 February 2014, further tests were conducted at the clinic by a contractor engaged by OP3 International Pte Ltd to check for leakages from the sump and water pipes.
On 8 March 2014, OP3 International Pte Ltd handed the clinic back to Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd after carrying out rectification works.
On 21 July 2014, Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd observed mould growth on the walls of the clinic for a second time following another flood originating at the clinic's drainage sump area.
On 22 July 2014, Ms Grace Chong, Managing Director of Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd, informed Mr Foo Kian Beng about the second flood at the clinic.
On 23 July 2014, Mr Foo Kian Beng visited the Smile Inc clinic to look into the circumstances surrounding the second flood.
On 28 and 29 August 2014, Mr Foo engaged a second contractor, AXN Engineering Pte Ltd, to conduct further hydrostatic tests on the Clinic’s water pipes.
On 28 and 29 August 2014, Mr Foo engaged a second contractor, AXN Engineering Pte Ltd, to conduct further hydrostatic tests on the Clinic’s water pipes.
On 15 December 2015, Mr Parwani advised Mr Foo by e-mail that OP3 had a strong defence to Smile Inc’s claim in Suit 498.
On 22 March 2016, OP3 engaged an independent expert, Mr Chee Yan Pong, to provide his opinion as to the cause of the First and Second Floods.
Mr Chee prepared a report dated 21 September 2016 setting out his views on the cause of the First and Second Floods.
On 10 December 2015, the director’s resolution authorised payment of a dividend of $1,200,000 to Mr Foo.
On 11 December 2015, OP3 International Pte Ltd paid a dividend of $1,200,000 to Mr Foo Kian Beng pursuant to the 10 December 2015 director's resolution.
On 10 December 2015, the director’s resolution authorised payment of a dividend of $700,000 to Mr Foo.
On 11 December 2015, OP3 International Pte Ltd paid a dividend of $700,000 to Mr Foo Kian Beng pursuant to the 10 December 2015 director's resolution.
On 8 July 2016, the director’s resolution authorised payment of a dividend of $400,000 to Mr Foo.
On 8 July 2016, as sole director of OP3 International Pte Ltd, Mr Foo Kian Beng signed a resolution authorising payment of a dividend of $400,000 to himself.
On 2017, OP3 paid Mr Foo $682,394 as repayment of moneys he had earlier loaned to the company.

On 10 February 2021, the liquidator of OP3 International Pte Ltd commenced HC/S 152/2021 in the name of OP3 International Pte Ltd against Mr Foo Kian Beng seeking to recover sums equivalent to payments made to him between 2015 and 2017.

On 20 January 2022, lawyer Vijai Parwani issued a letter stating that he had previously advised Mr Foo Kian Beng on 22 August 2014 and 25 May 2015 that Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd's claim in Suit 498 was unmeritorious.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether payments of dividends and loan repayments from OP3 International Pte Ltd to its sole director and shareholder during the period when the company faced a substantial claim from Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd should be reversed on the basis that they were made at times when the company’s financial position required its decision-maker to take into account the interests of creditors.
Party_Details.Rule: A company director must act in what the director considers to be the best interests of the company. When the company is approaching a state where it is likely to be unable to discharge its debts, the director’s assessment of the company’s interests must include having regard to the position of creditors and the effect of proposed transactions on the company’s ability to meet its obligations.
Party_Details.Application: OP3 International Pte Ltd entered into a contract in July 2013 to perform fitting out works for Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd and later faced a claim exceeding the original contract sum after two flooding incidents at the clinic. Smile Inc sent a letter of demand in August 2014 and served a writ and statement of claim in May 2015, and OP3 obtained legal and expert input about the dispute. During the period from December 2015 to 2017, while the Smile Inc claim remained unresolved, OP3 International Pte Ltd, acting through its sole director and shareholder, authorised and made multiple large dividend payments and loan repayments to that same individual. These transactions reduced the funds remaining in the company while a significant unresolved claim from a customer was outstanding, raising the issue of whether appropriate weight was given to creditors' interests when authorising the disputed transactions.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The nature, scope, and timing of the Creditor Duty as an aspect of a director’s fiduciary duty requires a two-stage inquiry: an objective assessment of the company’s financial state at the time of the impugned transaction, and a subjective examination of whether the director acted in the company’s best interests tested against that financial context. Financial states fall into three categories: (1) solvency, where shareholder interests proxy company interests; (2) an intermediate zone of imminent inability to discharge debts, requiring increasing weight to creditors’ interests; and (3) inevitable insolvency, where transactions exclusively benefiting shareholders breach the duty. A contingent liability, such as a pending high-value lawsuit, must be reasonably valued (discounting for litigation risk and overstatement). If this valuation renders the company balance sheet insolvent, the company operates in at least the intermediate zone. In such a state, authorizing substantial dividends and loan repayments to a sole shareholder-director without a legitimate corporate purpose beyond transferring value fails to give proper regard to creditors’ interests. Sparse, undetailed legal advice or informal auditor input does not justify ignoring a pending high-value claim for solvency assessments. The statutory rule on dividend payments and the fiduciary Creditor Duty operate as distinct, overlapping regimes. A breach of this fiduciary duty requires the restoration of the extracted funds to the company, and relief under the Companies Act is unavailable if the director did not act reasonably by failing to seek specific advice on the propriety of the large extractions given the pending claim.

Metadata.Coram: Sundaresh Menon CJ, Steven Chong JCA, Belinda Ang Saw Ean JCA, Kannan Ramesh JAD and Judith Prakash SJ
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Foo Kian Beng

Facts.Text: From the time of incorporation of OP3 International Pte Ltd in December 2006, Foo Kian Beng was its sole director and sole shareholder.
As sole director of OP3 International Pte Ltd, Foo Kian Beng oversaw completion of the fitting out works at the Smile Inc clinic and the handover on or about 31 October 2013.

After Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd notified OP3 International Pte Ltd on 17 January 2014 of a flood and mould at the clinic, Foo Kian Beng arranged for OP3 to carry out rectification works, including installing an access panel near the drainage sump area and engaging a contractor to check for leaks.
On 23 July 2014, after being informed of a second flood at the clinic, Foo Kian Beng went to the clinic to look into the situation.
Following the second flood, Foo Kian Beng engaged AXN Engineering Pte Ltd to conduct hydrostatic tests on the clinic's water pipes on 28 and 29 August 2014.

On 15 December 2015, lawyer Vijai Parwani of Parwani Law LLC emailed Foo Kian Beng stating that OP3 International Pte Ltd had a strong defence to Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd's claim in Suit 498.
On 22 March 2016, acting for OP3 International Pte Ltd, Foo Kian Beng engaged expert Mr Chee Yan Pong to provide an opinion on the causes of the flooding incidents at the Smile Inc clinic.

On 21 September 2016, expert Mr Chee Yan Pong delivered a report to OP3 International Pte Ltd, addressed to its management including Foo Kian Beng, setting out his views on the causes of the first and second floods.
On 22 August 2014 and 25 May 2015, according to a later letter, lawyer Vijai Parwani communicated advice to Foo Kian Beng that Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd's claim in Suit 498 was unmeritorious.
On 10 December 2015, as sole director of OP3 International Pte Ltd, Foo Kian Beng signed a resolution authorising payment of a dividend of $1,200,000 to himself.
On 11 December 2015, OP3 International Pte Ltd paid $1,200,000 to Foo Kian Beng as a dividend pursuant to the 10 December 2015 resolution.
On 10 December 2015, as sole director of OP3 International Pte Ltd, Foo Kian Beng signed a further resolution authorising payment of a dividend of $700,000 to himself.
On 11 December 2015, OP3 International Pte Ltd paid $700,000 to Foo Kian Beng as a dividend pursuant to the 10 December 2015 resolution.
On 11 December 2015, OP3 International Pte Ltd paid $138,352 to Foo Kian Beng as repayment of money he had previously loaned to the company.
On 8 July 2016, as sole director of OP3 International Pte Ltd, Foo Kian Beng signed a resolution authorising payment of a dividend of $400,000 to himself.
On 13 July 2016, OP3 International Pte Ltd paid $400,000 to Foo Kian Beng as a dividend.
On 27 December 2016, OP3 International Pte Ltd recorded a director's resolution for a dividend of $500,000 in favour of Foo Kian Beng.
In 2017, OP3 International Pte Ltd paid a total of $682,394 to Foo Kian Beng as repayment of money he had previously loaned to the company.

On 20 January 2022, lawyer Vijai Parwani issued a letter stating that he had advised Foo Kian Beng on 22 August 2014 and 25 May 2015 that Smile Inc Dental Surgeons Pte Ltd's claim in Suit 498 was unmeritorious.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a sole director is personally liable to repay funds received from a company as dividends and loan repayments if the transactions were authorized at a time when the company was insolvent due to a pending legal claim.
Party_Details.Rule: A director’s duty to act in the best interests of the company shifts to include creditors once the company enters an 'intermediate zone' of financial distress. In this zone, directors cannot prioritize their own interests as shareholders over the interests of creditors. To avoid liability, a director must show they acted reasonably, which typically requires seeking specific professional advice on the impact of large fund extractions on the company’s ability to satisfy pending claims.
Party_Details.Application: Foo Kian Beng authorized substantial payments to himself while OP3 faced a $1.5 million claim from Smile Inc. While he relied on a brief email from litigation counsel asserting a 'strong defence' and an expert report on causation, these materials did not address the financial prudence of extracting almost $1.2 million from the company while the claim was outstanding. By the end of 2016, once the Smile Inc claim is reasonably valued as a contingent liability, the company’s assets were insufficient to cover its debts. The authorization of a $500,000 dividend and the subsequent $682,394 loan repayment in 2017 occurred while the company was in this state of distress.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: A director who authorizes the extraction of the bulk of a company's cash assets for their own benefit while a significant lawsuit is pending fails to give proper weight to the interests of creditors. Sparse and undetailed legal advice describing a defence as 'strong' does not justify a director ignoring a high-value claim when assessing solvency. Furthermore, a director who does not seek specific accounting or legal advice on the impact of such distributions on creditors cannot be said to have acted reasonably. Since the payments in late 2016 and 2017 depleted the company during a period of balance-sheet insolvency, the director is personally liable to restore those funds to the company. The failure to appreciate the risk posed to creditors by these specific extractions constitutes a breach of fiduciary duty.
